questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive allows forced removal of CPU from running process
Non-preemptive lets process run to completion or voluntary yield
Example preemptive: Round Robin, SRTF
Example non-preemptive: FCFS, Non-preemptive SJF

questions.question: Define Turnaround Time and Waiting Time. How are they related to Burst Time and Arrival Time?
keyPoints: Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time
Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time
Burst Time is actual CPU execution time needed
Relationship shows how long process waits relative to its execution needs

questions.question: What is the Convoy Effect in FCFS scheduling? How does it affect system performance?
keyPoints: Convoy Effect occurs when short processes wait behind long processes
Caused by CPU-bound processes blocking I/O-bound processes
Results in low CPU utilization and poor throughput
All processes suffer from high waiting time

questions.question: Explain the concept of Aging in process scheduling. How does it help in preventing starvation?
keyPoints: Aging gradually increases priority of waiting processes
Prevents starvation of low-priority long-waiting processes
Based on the principle: longer wait = higher priority
Ensures fairness in priority scheduling systems

questions.question: What is Context Switching? Discuss its impact on CPU scheduling performance.
keyPoints: Context switching saves/restores CPU state when switching processes
Involves saving registers, PC, and process control blocks
Higher frequency of context switches increases overhead
Affects throughput and CPU efficiency negatively

questions.question: Calculate average waiting time for processes: P1(AT=0, BT=7), P2(AT=1, BT=4), P3(AT=2, BT=1), P4(AT=3, BT=4) using SJF (non-preemptive).
keyPoints: Process execution order: P1, P3, P2, P4
P1 waiting time: 0, P3 waiting time: 6, P2 waiting time: 6, P4 waiting time: 10
Total waiting time: 22
Average waiting time: 22/4 = 5.5

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Round Robin scheduling?
keyPoints: Advantages: Fair distribution, good response time, no starvation
Disadvantages: High context switch overhead, large waiting time for long processes, performance depends on time quantum

questions.question: Consider the following processes: P1(AT=0, BT=10), P2(AT=1, BT=5), P3(AT=2, BT=8), P4(AT=3, BT=6). Calculate average waiting time and turnaround time for: (a) FCFS (b) SJF (non-preemptive).
keyPoints: FCFS order: P1,P2,P3,P4 - Calculate completion times and derive TAT, WT
SJF order: P1,P2,P4,P3 (shortest burst first after P1)
Show that SJF gives lower average waiting time

questions.question: Explain Multilevel Queue Scheduling and Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling. How do they differ? What are the advantages of MLFQ over MLQ?
keyPoints: MLQ: Fixed queues for different process types with separate scheduling algorithms
MLFQ: Processes can move between queues based on CPU burst behavior
MLFQ advantages: Flexibility, adaptability, prevents starvation
MLFQ allows CPU-bound processes to drop to lower priority, I/O-bound to stay high

questions.question: Using Round Robin with time quantum = 4, calculate the execution sequence and average waiting time for: P1(AT=0, BT=10), P2(AT=1, BT=5), P3(AT=2, BT=8).
keyPoints: Completion times: P1=22, P2=8, P3=26
Turnaround: P1=22, P2=7, P3=24
Average waiting time = 30/3 = 10

questions.question: Discuss the various criteria used for evaluating CPU scheduling algorithms. Which criteria are most important for: (i) Batch systems (ii) Interactive systems
keyPoints: Criteria: CPU utilization, Throughput, Turnaround time, Waiting time, Response time
Batch systems: Maximize throughput, minimize turnaround and waiting time
Interactive systems: Minimize response time, ensure fairness
Real-time systems: Meet deadlines, predictability
